title: INJECAGENT: Benchmarking Indirect Prompt Injections in Tool-Integrated Large Language Model Agents
contributions_summary: INJECAGENT introduces the first comprehensive benchmark for evaluating indirect prompt injection (IPI) attacks on tool-integrated LLM agents, comprising 1,054 realistic test cases spanning 17 user tools and 62 attacker tools across finance, smart home, and email domains, categorized into direct harm and data exfiltration intents. Through systematic evaluation of 30 LLM agents, the study reveals that ReAct-prompted models like GPT-4 are highly vulnerable, with attack success rates reaching 24% in base settings and nearly doubling to 47% when reinforced with hacking prompts, while fine-tuned agents demonstrate significantly greater resilience (e.g., GPT-4 fine-tuned at 7.1% ASR). The work further identifies that attack success is predominantly driven by user case characteristics—particularly high-content-freedom placeholders that allow malicious instructions to blend seamlessly—and demonstrates that enhanced settings with explicit hacking prompts amplify agent sensitivity, revealing critical trade-offs between attack efficacy and defensive awareness, thereby establishing a foundational framework for future security research and mitigation strategies in real-world LLM agent deployments.
